reviews.review_text: I don't even want another Elder Scrolls game anymore.... please give us another Dawnguard style Skyrim DLC lord Howard

At 430 hours, plus several hundred more on other devices, how could I NOT love Skyrim? It baffles me that there are gamers who still haven't touched this game. Without mods, you have this open world that you can explore endlessly with some bugs that may force a restart. With mods? You've just unlocked the true potential for open world RPGs and frankly, in this man's opinion, it doesn't get any better than this right here! What are you reading this for? Go play it!

>buy it after 15 years of piracy
>play vanilla for 10 minutes
>download 2000 mods
>relive childhood

These sands are cold, but Khajiit feels warmness from your presence
10/10

Where to begin, my friend had convinced me to play this game.Because I had never played this game when it first came out last year in 2025.It was the first time I ever played and what did I really think of it? I really loved it.I don't know why I never played this game back in 2010s when it came out, it was confusing and I didn't know what to do.But after playing it for some time, I was able to get used to playing the game i played regular first and then I played it with mods and honestly.I enjoyed playing modded skyrim the most then playing at regularly being able to use my favorite custom follower Xelzaz i would explain more about the other followers I had. But I'd rather not. It will take forever honestly, I am going to miss playing this game. For awhile  i have been playing the game for eleven months.Straight ever since I started playing it.But now I can finally decide to take a break from it.Finally, once and for all, for a very long time, I am going to explain it.And I will still continue to play it on certain occasions, but until then, I will give this game, I say an A i mean, just because of the story.And gameplay and I will definitely be playing this again.One day and starting the whole journey over again. I'm glad to be a part of the community once and for all, and I'm hoping to continue this journey, hopefully 1 day again in the future. Goodbye, skyrim.I hope to see you again soon. 🧡🖤

Mamma mia, what a friggin jank fiesta. That's Bethesda for ya I guess.

Thought I was gonna be bored to hell and be overwhelmed by all its RPG elements, but it was surprisingly straightforward and I became quite invested in the story. Overall, enjoyable stuff despite the jank and ♥♥♥♥♥. I might come back and do more stuff than the main quest line later, but for now I'm satisfied with just that. I ended my playthrough by buying a place in Whiterun and I adopted Lucia. Very wholesome, thanks Todd.

But it's Skyrim resold to me again, lol.

It also appears there is no native Workshop support.  Because, mods are locked behind a $20 DLC.

They took out mods so they can sell it to you on a resold game.  

Weirdos.

[h1]A Long Journey I Didn’t Want to End[/h1]

[b]“What is better, to be born good, or to overcome your evil nature through great effort?”[/b]

I love Skyrim more than I ever expected to. It doesn’t just give you content, it gives you a world that slowly becomes familiar, like you’ve actually spent time living in it. There is just so much packed in here, lore, characters, quests, dragons, magic, factions, followers, and random encounters that still catch me off guard even after hours of play. I’m 214 hours in, and I genuinely enjoyed almost every second of it, not because it was perfect, but because it always felt alive.

My playstyle turned into pure curiosity. I turned into the kind of player who says “I’ll just do this one quest” and then ends up clearing an entire town, looting every cave nearby, and picking up three more quests on the way back. I explored everywhere I could reach, from snowy mountains to hidden caves and forgotten ruins, just to see what was there. I tried every weapon type I could find, switched between magic, archery, and melee, and messed around with shouts just because it felt fun in the moment. I made good decisions, bad decisions, and sometimes completely chaotic ones just to see how the world would react. That freedom never stopped feeling satisfying.

What really hit me over time is how personal the experience becomes. The game starts turning into your own story, shaped by what you choose to do and what you ignore. Some quests stick with you more than others, some moments feel surprisingly emotional, and then suddenly you’re thrown into something strange or silly that breaks the tension in a way only Skyrim can. It’s that mix of serious storytelling and random unpredictability that made the world feel real to me in a weird way. I also found myself going deeper into the lore outside the game, reading about the history of cities, Daedric Princes, past events, weapons, and characters. The more I learned, the more the world inside the game felt alive, and the people in it started to feel real too, like they all had their own lives, histories, and reasons for being where they are.

At this point, I don’t even feel like I “finished” Skyrim. It feels more like I paused a long journey I’ve been slowly living through. I still remember small moments more clearly than I expected, random fights, quiet walks through the wilderness, unexpected quests that went in directions I didn’t expect. It all adds up in a way that feels bigger than the main story itself.

I used to think people just called it their favorite game because it was popular or nostalgic, but after actually putting real time into it, I completely get it now. [u]It’s not just a game you play, it’s a world you spend time in, and I’m honestly proud to say Skyrim became my favorite game I’ve ever played.[/u]

There hasn’t been a Dragonborn in Tamriel for hundreds of years, until you came along.

The Dark Brotherhood haven’t had a Listener for years, until you came along.
  
The Thieves Guild have had the same leader for 25 years, until you came along. 

The Psijic Order haven’t made an appearance in a hundred years, until you came along. 

The Companions haven’t had a true leader since Ysgramor in thousands of years, until you came along.

Serana was locked away for thousands of years, until you came along. 

Miraak stayed in Hermaeus Mora’s dimension for over 4,000 years, until you came along. 
 
You came along, but nobody still gives a ♥♥♥♥ about the Bard’s college.

There's something to be said about playing a game 10 years later again and feeling like it's brand new. This game is carried by it's players to the point where the game itself was reinvented. That being said, the engine is quirky, there are few paths to create your own mods but the developers did provide us with the creation kit. So if we strip all that away and just look at the base game with DLC, it's solid still. I think it's one of those things in life where you just have to experience it atleast once. This in my book is a cult classic.

of course, a classic, I played a lot on console so I played probably at least 200 hours really, I don't play it a lot but that's just me, there is a lot of potential, maybe just a liitle bit laggy sometimes but nothing really unplayable on my side
what can I say that hasn't been said already??? the game is 15 year old and still has one of the strongest modding communities around!

The game has aged incredibly well and, because of that, it turned Bethesda into a lazy, money-hungry company. They barely release any meaningful updates anymore; the game is kept alive by a passionate community that loves modding. And every time Bethesda sees a cool mod idea, they either take it and add it as if it was their own or release some update nobody asked for that ends up breaking half the game.

But that's just my opinion after playing this game across multiple editions and platforms: PS3, PS4, PS5, Epic Games (which was awful because I couldn't install decent mods), a pirated version back when I couldn't afford to buy yet another copy of the game on PC, where I could finally use mods that gave it a new life, and eventually the Steam version.

If you've never played it, I absolutely recommend it. But if you're one of those people with a 30-minute attention span for games, you'll probably hate it. The game gives you a lot of freedom and isn't one of those modern titles filled with UI elements constantly telling you what to do, how to do it, and where to go with some ultra-interactive map holding your hand the entire time.

That being said, it's about time Bethesda released another Elder Scrolls instead of selling us the same damn remake or remaster over and over again.

Barebones boring game. 
Dont get me wrong i really tried liking this game after a decade of info and memes and everything upselling this game as the ONE, i tried it, didnt like it. Before any of you start bashing me and start telling me that Rpgs are not meant for me and go on a rant defending this game. I have played a lot of open world games that are Rpgs and this game might be revolutionary and enjoyable if i got it back then when it came out but that is questionable even then, i know because i recently played the Witcher 1 that came out way before this game and i liked it to the very end.
Skyrim V is lackluster and shallow. I dont know what else to tell you, you might try it for yourself and like it. The game has a lot of quests that dont feel very rewarding when you finish them, sending you to fetch something on the complete oposite side of the map. If you like doing that in games and playing with mods then sure go for it, but wait for sale.

Don't see what the hype is about, I liked playing it but it actually felt less entertaining than Oblivion and when my save stopped working I didn't feel like starting again. But if you like this type of game obviously you'll love this.

If you are a fan of RPGs, then Skyrim is absolutely a must-play game. Even after all these years, it still manages to deliver countless hours of entertainment whether you are playing completely vanilla or diving deep into the massive modding community. There is always something to do, somewhere new to explore, or another quest waiting around the corner.

One of the biggest strengths of Skyrim is the freedom it gives the player. You can become a warrior charging into battle with a two-handed sword, a stealthy assassin lurking in the shadows, a powerful mage controlling the battlefield, or honestly whatever kind of character you want to create. The world feels huge and alive, filled with caves, ruins, cities, dragons, guilds, and random encounters that can easily pull you away from your original objective for hours at a time.

The modding community also deserves a huge amount of praise. Mods can completely transform the experience with improved graphics, new quests, combat overhauls, survival mechanics, companions, and even entirely new lands to explore. At the same time, Skyrim is still incredibly enjoyable without mods, which says a lot about the quality of the base game itself.

That being said, the game is not perfect. One thing that stands out after long play sessions is the repetitive NPC dialogue. Eventually it starts to feel like you are hearing the same lines over and over again from different characters. In my opinion, The Elder Scrolls III: Morrowind had much stronger and more memorable dialogue interactions. Skyrim shines more through exploration and gameplay than deep conversations with characters.

Still, despite that flaw, Skyrim remains one of the most entertaining RPGs ever made. The Elder Scrolls series has always been known for creating immersive fantasy worlds, and Skyrim continues that tradition perfectly. Between the exploration, character progression, soundtrack, quests, dragons, and nearly endless replayability, this is easily a game worth adding to your collection.

Even years later, Skyrim is still a game you can lose yourself in for hundreds of hours and somehow still find new things you missed before.

One of my favorite games of all time.
  This game is a open world RPG that can be first person or third person POV.
No classes like most other games. Build your character how you want.
The world is immersive and has a lot of mod support so you can change up the game as much as you want.

Skyrim is one of my favorite games of all time. An epic RPG fantasy from Bethesda that is still relevant 15 years later. I first played this game in high school, and it had so much content it took me almost a year to play all the way through. There is always something to do, and there's something for everyone in this immersive adventure. I love this game so much and have played it so many times.

I'm sick I didn't play this game when It first came out when I was younger. there is so much to do and learn it's insane and the meme lore just from this game alone that you don't realize is so prevalent in todays media is mind blowing. great game to play when your young and unemployed 10/10 will continue to play and probably play again.
Skyrim is a Soul Prison with no exit. Once you taste it, there are nothing better like Skyrim, even 25 or 30 years after the game launched

VALE A PENA EM 2026?

Absolutamente. Skyrim é o melhor jogo já feito na história, você pode ser absolutamente TUDO o que você quiser e criar sua própria história. É muita coisa, parece complicado, mas não é. O jogo inteiro é extremamente intuitivo e relaxante, os personagens são carismáticos e você sente como se nunca estivesse sozinho – especialmente porque você pode escolher um NPC para ser sua companhia o jogo todo – você pode casar, adotar filhos, comprar casa, comprar cavalos, brigar no bar, ir para a prisão, explorar lugares absurdos, entrar para uma ceita, entrar para escola de magia, e seu pc pode ser tão potente quanto uma batata: a experiência será incrível, mesmo com gráficos antigos, o capricho enorme faz com que você sinta que são extremamente avançados. A trilha sonora é envolvente e absoluta, nostálgica. Se você gosta de dark fantasy, esse é o melhor jogo possível. A facilidade de Skyrim está na sua maneira de jogar, você consegue usar uma mão para cada arma e criar combinações de build do jeito que quiser. Não perca tempo e jogue Skyrim.
Jogo essa bomba desde de 2013 na versão pirata, e agora platinado na steam! E Sempre descobrindo coisas do game até hoje.
Definitely one of the best, if not the best, open world RPGs out there. This game is Bethesda at its best. Explore, plot, mod, and research your heart away to a great gaming experience.

[b]SKYRIM IS PEACE.[/b]

When I think of [i]The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim,[/i] I think of the countless hours I've spent playing this game on a multitude of devices, including my xbox console, my laptop, my tower PC, my previous tower PC, and my friend's xbox.
No other game makes me come back for the sole reason of experiencing it [i]even without any mods,[/i] perhaps aside [i]Fallout 4.[/i] You may call me a huge fan of Bethesda, but really, I've only played these two so far. And [i]Starfield.[/i] We don't talk about [i]Starfield.[/i]

[hr][/hr]
[b]SKYRIM IS A GAME.[/b]

Skyrim as a game is near perfect to me. It combines true freedom (including the freedom to modify) with an unbeatable sense of immersion, a sense of belonging, and of course a perfect soundtrack into a piece of art that shaped years of my life and decisions I've made along the way. That includes but is not limited to financial and career choices.
Yet I sometimes question how important a game can be to a person, someone like me. Though it is not reality, it has had real impact on me and therefore the people around me. Hardly a day goes by where I don't remind myself of the life lessons and memes that I have collected from this game.

[hr][/hr]
[b]SKYRIM IS A WORLD.[/b]

I'm sure to most who have played this game – and even some who haven't – it is common knowledge that "Skyrim" is the name of the country/region where the game plays in.
To me, as one who lives in the lands of Germany and now too Sweden, Skyrim holds a very special place in my heart in terms of familiarity. Not only do the landscapes remind me of home, they also bring with them a fresh, mystical nature that I used to imagine while listening to my parents' tales and reading children's books. 
[i]
"Large evergreen trees and unfathomably tall mountains, the falling of unforgiving snow, and grey rocks that spread across all fields and streets."[/i]

[hr][/hr]
[b]SKYRIM IS A FEELING.[/b]

"The Skyrim Feeling" cannot be a unique state of mind that is solely limited to me. I refuse to believe that.
Millions of people have played this game, felt it resonate within them, felt the becoming of [i]Dovahkiin.[/i] And I am one of them. I wake up in a world of ice and dragons and choose to be the person I am and wander into the mist. I hear the somber and empowering instruments and the echoes of a choir, they follow me as a take thousands of steps. I am carried by purpose that only I am aware of – when I shout in game, my heart in real life shouts as well. 
You, who has seen Skyrim; you have felt the same, have you not?

I am convinced that Skyrim would have been half its success without Jeremy Soule. What he composed, what he brought to life, is priceless to me. Watch me yap about the soundtrack for hours and hours.

[hr][/hr]
[b]SKYRIM IS NOT PERFECT.[/b]

For all the amazing memories I have gathered in Skyrim, I do have some criticism, some unpleasant moments I'm sure most players share.
Dying and having to load a save from half an hour ago, getting stuck in some stupid rock hole, and seeing all my books vanish from the bookshelf in my house. Just to name a few. And that barely covers the surface of my frustration.
I could be cliché about it and say "well, that's just part of Skyrim's beauty" – and while that certainly is true, I must insist on being somewhat annoyed by it, as a game designer, as a human being.
I know the game is quite dated by now, and whatever excuse one may come up with; I don't want to hear it. Trust me, I know.

It is fine to be imperfect. I am still here loving the game, am I not?

[hr][/hr]
[b]SKYRIM IS HOME.[/b]

What is Skyrim in one word, if not [i]home?[/i] Home is defined by many things: a place to be able to rest at, a place where all your loved ones gather, a place where you place all your trust in, to lend you a moment of peace from the chaos of the world.
That is Skyrim to me. Not my only home, but definitely one of them.

Afterword: 
[i]My loved ones do not gather in Skyrim, but they – my best friends – do gather because of it.[/i]

I keep uninstalling Skyrim… and it keeps coming back like nothing happened.

Every time I swear I’m done, I end up reinstalling it and making a new stealth archer again. Mods or vanilla, it doesn’t matter—this game just refuses to die.

10/10 eternal addiction.

I never actually beat this game when it first came out, but I put a ton of hours into it just exploring. This time around I'm aiming to beat the actual main storyline. Having access to some of the DLC makes the side-questing even more diverse and I'm enjoying myself. I know this specific version of the game isn't well received but I don't know the actual details behind it. I just know I'm having a great time playing!

Bought it to compensate 500+ h of playtime on a pirate version. 
Wish Bethesda could learn from it's previous games so it could keep on cooking good games. 
But at the end... We got StArFiElD *sigh* what a miracle...

THE ONLY GAME I NEVER TRULY UNINSTALL!  Say what you want about this game but it is the only game that I found myself constantly coming back to year after year.  The community is the real MVP here.  Thanks to the incredible variety of mods available, Skyrim SE remains fresh, beautiful and truly enjoyable even in 2026,  For me, it's easily one of the greatest games ever made--not just for what's in the box, but for the decade of love and support the community has poured into it.  If you want a game that will literally never run out of content, this is it.  P.S. I recently restarted my Skyrim journey again with Canliberk's I&A Collection, I've already added another 60hrs to my tally and absolutely loving it!  If you're looking for a way to overhaul your experience, defintely check this one out.
This is one of the best open worlds that you will find, once you have completed all of the content in the game there are mods that will keep it fresh for as long as you want to play it.
Honestly with its core game and mod support this is the only game you'll ever need.

I like morrowind and oblivion a little more but this game is still phenomenal and has aspects that are done in better ways than its predecessors, even if they stripped too many systems back for my liking. RIP levitate mark recall spellcrafting attributes athletics and acrobatics.

OH! nevermind! my entire PC is unable to be interfaced with! its not frozen, but my mouse is unable to click anything. Let me close skyrim to figure out a solution to that. Oh! even after COMPLETELY closing both skyrim AND steam, my pc is still broken! tried many fixes, truly no solution. Breaks my pc entirely.

Remains the only game I can start a new save file on and get the same amount of enjoyment as my first playthrough. 11/10 greatest RPG ever released and none have yet to dethrone it in my eyes.

A top sandbox RPG. The only noticeable issue is the enemy level-scaling system; other than that, the game is fantastic.
9.6/10
The game that I played the most in my entire life, now that I have a decent PC might add another couple thousand hours to it. Will have plenty of time since ESO 6 will be released in about 10 years.

Skyrim is by far the best game I’ve ever played, yes it has it’s flaws, no it isn’t completely perfect, but this game completely changed my life. I would not be the person I am today had it not been for Skyrim, this game was always there for me in my worst moments and it changed me for the better. No matter how many times I play this game (and no matter how many copies I buy) I never get bored with it. I recommend everyone to ever live to experience this game at least once.

I honestly come back to this game every year and play it for a few hundred hours all over again. I just wish they would update the graphics, skill perk trees, and the magic system. graphics still hold up just would be nice to have an update. skill perks could be greatly expanded upon to give the game a new and improved feel to it, and magic does not scale well, so after a certain level mage builds become useless. That being said I compare every game I play to this one here. the closest that has come is red dead redemption 2, for obvious reasons. Also would love to see an update to the Werewolf in this game. again after a certain level it greatly loses power. When it should be relevant throughout the game, as it is a werewolf. Personal opinion but werewolves should have some customization to them as well, glowing eyes. different fur color that sort of thing.
I've played the game since I was a little kid an no matter what point I was at I constantly turn back to it for more challenges and just fun.
It's Skyrim, it's good. Just sucks that it's over 10 years old with no sequel in sight. Also Todd needs to stop taking all my money by making me buy Skyrim again.

Good game. Anniversary Edition was worth the money. If I forgot everything about Skyrim, I would want my friend Zack to convince me to play it again. With my small 200+ hours on this game, I went out of my way and got every achievement. This game is really fun, especially when you have other people helping you out when you need help with puzzles, especially if they already know the game. Absolutely amazing game for 2011. Thank you Todd Howard.

skyrim is an extremely well known game and even for it being re4leased when it was it still outshines games released now. the customization, hours of gameplay, and replayability make this game shine. it has something for everyone truly and suits many different play styles.
Played for five hours and still haven't launched a new game, just been absolutely obliterating my PC with as many mods as possible to make my own ideal elder scrolls 6 that i'll never play.
Incredible detail, music and immersion. Its a must play.

Helgen also has to be one of the best intros to any game I've ever played. But above all else, this game is amazing because you can kill that motherf***er Nazeem.

Even without mods or all of the Creation Club stuff, Skyrim is a 10/10. Just buy it, play it, and once you get comfortable, go crazy with the mods!
In my personal opinion Oblivion had much stronger writing and unique quest but to this day Skyrim is hands down the best game to run a character idea in your head and just see how it would work.

I have played this game for countless hours since it came out across multiple xbox, laptops, and now my PC. There really is nothing else like it. My only complaint is that I am worried Bethesda won't be able to make TES:VI as perfect.

Skyrim is such a huge game in terms of world size, story, and the iconic place it holds for people experiencing entertainment in this generation.  So, it is very difficult to describe in a few lines what I personally liked or disliked about it. If you enjoy video games and haven't tried Skyrim yet, then I strongly suggest you play it for at least a few hours before you decide it's for you, or not.  It's immersive and fun,  but also very dark. Very few of the choices you make in each questline affect the outcome of any other questline, and I disliked that, but I'd still recommend the game.  There's just so much to explore and do, that you never run out of adventures or new things to see.

Over 1,000 hours since 2015 and skyrim still refuses to age. The base game has unmatched atmosphere, but the modding community is what makes it immortal lol. Im currently 120+ hours into a fresh playthorugh with 200+ mods installed and it literally feels like a modern RPG. You just never truly leave this game.

Open world is nice. Some things do not get explained unless you talk to the NPC's so be aware of that especially if they are a shop owner. The map is a little frustrating as it's very limited with details. Otherwise so far I'm enjoying playing it.

If only we have the ability to force skooma addiction to these damned yellow-bellied elves, I'd give Todd more money.

Gave the game thousands of hours on the Legendary Edition, revisiting the game on Special Edition is worth it. Never would've thought it's more stable than LE, surprisingly enough. Hundreds of hours on quests, a thousand more by the modding community.

And by the nine... the modding community is nuts. Try not to go overboard. Remember what happened to Kagrenac: He installed too many cheat mods and his Numidium OS got bricked.
love the game im running it with 2k graphic mods on a core i5 4570 3.6ghz 16gb ram and a nvidia geforce 750 4gb

It's Skyrim - it's the most branded and re-branded game that you've bought 4 or 5 times for a reason and why I'm playing it again years after it was released.

See it on sale for a platform you don't yet own it on? Get it again lol
Finally got the time to play this. This game brings the RPG gameplay to the next level. I wish there could be more games like this
its a good game even with a pretty crappy computer it runs well, it doesn't stop it slows when in extreme lag
I ran this game well i averged about 60 fps with no worries or bad 0.1 percent lows I would reccomend getting this its rlly fun there are alot of diverse builts to choose from.
SKYRIM IS AMAZING free mods in game, unquie storyline, beautiful graphics and great gameplay, honsetly best game ive ever played, worth my money.
It's a relaxed game where you can mostly do what you want to. There are some bugs but that doesn't matter.

Truly a really GREAT game, it really lived up to the hype i heared about it. I just really wish they either make another game like skyrim or make it with modern engine and fix some of the stuff. I do mod it to some extent but if u wanna mod the graphics for it to look really modern its pretty hard and sometimes breaks. Overall i really love playing this and would recommend.

The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim is one of the greatest RPGs ever made. It offers a massive open world full of exploration, quests, and freedom. Every playthrough feels unique because you can choose your own path—whether you become a warrior, mage, thief, or simply explore the world at your own pace.

The main story is enjoyable, but what truly makes Skyrim special is its side quests, exploration, and the incredible modding community that keeps the game alive even after so many years.

While the graphics and AI are outdated compared to modern games, the depth, freedom, and atmosphere more than make up for it.

Verdict:
Skyrim is not just a game, it’s a world you can live in. Highly recommended for every RPG fan.

This was my first Elder Scrolls game and I can't get enough of it. I love the questlines and the ability to play in whatever way I could possibly want. The only limit is what you yourself decide to do. It is probably my favorite gaming experience I've ever had, and I haven't even started modding yet. Peak.

Is it showing its age? Yes.
Is it still fun to play? Yes, if you paid 5 bucks for it.
It's certainly a time killer. Perfect for casual gameplay, but nothing immerging.
Do you see the Witcher for the same price? Get that one.

Simple, classic, Skyrim is what it is. It's been around forever and even though the Bethesda bugs are definitely there (half of Whiterun loads as basic model texture underneath maybe 40% of the time I return, trolls floating in the air etc - when you embrace them, they just become an easter egg, lol); its solid and was my first venture into open world gaming and even games like this, so it hits a soft spot.

I have been playing this for years and i always find myself coming back. I had it on the xbox and used that disk until it was practically fried. Im excited to have it on steam so i can immortalize my badass character

"You've played for 6 hours."

My sweet summer child. I've spent more than that installing 217 mods.

Not to mention the 1500+ hours across three playthroughs on a version of the game that was, let's say, less than purchased.

Yeah, I recommend it.

A amazing game. A massive amounts of exploring available. it has a great community of mod makers so it doesn't really end with new stuff being made. Still fun and one of my all time favorites of all time

Even though I've played this game for hundreds of hours on PS3 and PS4, I decided to give it a shot on PC since it was on sale for $13. It runs on ultra settings on my 2018 MacBook Pro and it looks so much better than on console!

a Classic. Replayablity is Amazing. Hoping to see Elder Scrolls 6 Soon. Hope it won't be in UE 5

Despite all the critics you really can just mod out all the problems I mention later. Skyrim really is a gem that is relaxing and accessible. It is a fantasy game you turn your brain off and do random stuff with. It looks the good without mods and is the best looking elder scrolls games and mods you can make it look like modern with the same style which isnt so simple for older bethesda games with lower poly count characters and worlds. It has added back some nice like less fast travel and transportation. You also get a good sense of the races cultures through gameplay. Even though the lesser holds kinda of suck all the walled city have a unique look. Every holds environment is quite unique looking too. 

The biggest difference between skyrim and the other elder scrolls games is the removal of attributes. They also fallout-ified aspects skills with a perk system. The perk system is functional in some cases like sleath or melee or crappy like magic perk tree. The biggest problem is there are too many filler perks like a perk that can be upgraded 5 times or a perk that only helps for expert level. Filler perks like are extra bad since they basically lock your chracter out of the reward for hitting mastery. Which really hurts the formula imporve a skill by doing. There is also no way to remove perks unless you 100 a skill just to reset it. Which is super time consuming and soft locks alot of players build every other playthrough.

The perk system only starts to feel less like a hinderance when using the perk tree for crafting. Smithing Enchanting and potion brewing are really powerful even without exploits. Doubling or trippling damage and armor rating with potions and enchantments that boost smithing saves you alot of perks you would have to have spent on damage or armor rating improvement perks. Enchanting also let you do even more damage, regenerate stats, or have a higher amount of stats that help deal more damage. So outside of duel weilding stamina perks cost reduction perks arent needed either. It is also easy to reset smithing alchemy and enchanting since it easy to level it to 100 if you the 5-10 points back.  So almost every build needs crafting. 

Sneak is such a good perk tree and its awesome that it is a strong playstyle. Dagger and bows actually have a niche unique to the other wepons now. Duel wielding, shields, and two handed give melee some varity they did not have before. There is a problem with the fact that sword mace and axe and the two handed version are not mechanically rich and are not that balanced. The effects never come into play and the slower swing speed means swords are better. Duel wielding swords or sword dagger is the only build head and shoulders better than the other for melee. Not being able to block is a non factor and having to enchanted wepons gives it way more utility than other builds. Two handed is not bad per say it just does do much other than have a better power attack against blocks and shields have unique enchants, perks. and stronger bash but you really dont need the block and stagger system with higher dps. Magic was absolutely gutted in this game outside of alteration and fire magic mages are low damage are low health. 300 armor from ebonflesh is too low and dragonskins cost and cast time is too high mage is unplayable without armor or the auto cast flesh spell perk. There are very few ways to increase damage as mage too and mana runs out so quickly its unplayable without a spell reduction or mana regen build. There one mask in the game and potions as the only source of increasing your damage. Its kinda of cool that magic bypasses blocking but this class has to play super meta and repetitive and is still kinda weak. It feels like non magic or hybrid focused character get more out of it than pure mages since there are some powerful perks and birth stones. 

It cool that quests and factions are more localized to holds. Though there are alot of radient quests in this game and its unfortunate the factions are limited to only 6 quest that arent radient when the had 12-16 in oblivion and morrowind. The dlc quests and main quests are actually pretty good. I think the civil war questline and many other faction questlines had more time. The civil war setting is the first time in a while that elder scrolls lore and written was actually a part of the gameplay instead of stuff hidden in the games books.
Its actually so good even after like 15 years after release its still so good

It's Skyrim. What more do I need to say. The game is renowned for making you feel like you're part of the world of Skyrim. Choices have consequences and NPCs have their own daily schedules and leave their posts to return to their own places of residence. Mod support is nearly unparalleled. Overall it's an excellent RPG riddled with Bethesda's "charm" that is the various bugs and exploits that can be found throughout the game. Very much so recommend to anyone who has yet to play this gem.
Till here i´m liking the game, i like the medieval stuff and other stuff, the animations and variety, i guess ill play more often this game, sincerly in my words, i recommend, God bless everybody.
This game is amazing you can explore trade git  loot and kill dragons and be dragon born you can get married you can adopt children and so much more  i realy recomend playing this

Played it a bunch of times and am going through it on PC now. I just played Hogwarts Legacy right before this, and although the graphics were really nice and the combat was pretty fun, the story and quests were pretty much garbage. It had been a while since I'd played Skyrim, so I went back to my current playthrough. Immediately, I did some random side quest that had more depth than 99% of the quests in Hogwarts Legacy, and it just goes to show that the age of a game does not necessarily make it worse. One of the greatest games of all time.

Man what the hell do I have to even say about this game? As of typing this review it has the most hours put into it out of my whole library. It's ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ Skyrim, it's definitely more streamlined and simpler compared to its predecessor Oblivion, but this game endures for as long as it did for good reasons.

CRAZY MODDING SCENE:
It's got a very extensive and active modding community that's advanced enough to convert the game's genre into a damn souls-like experience (*cough* *cough* or something inappropriately adult *cough* *cough*). New npcs, expansions and remodels for pretty much everything, DLC-quality quest mods that rivals that of the game's official content, reworks of mechanics or addition of entirely new ones, and even ACCURATE BOOB PHYSICS AND COLLISIONS. This highly advanced and seemingly everlasting modding scene can justify a pc purchase of this game alone tbh. I'm sure I spent more time scrolling through the Nexus mods page than actually playing the game.

THE MAIN THING I LOVED:
Modding aside, my absolute favorite thing about the vanilla game I deeply adore is the world and its atmosphere. The environments are top-notch and aged like fine wine, even with the game's aged fidelity in graphics its still stunning and the art direction holds up well today. And I believe the Special Edition added new graphical effects like sun ray beams, which only added to the game's beauty. The region of Skyrim is a cold, rugged place, and the game's style, soundscape, and ambience catches that feel really well, but what made it perfect to me is Jeremy Soule's ever timeless soundtrack for the game, I and many who enjoyed Skyrim and the other Elderscrolls games undoubtedly heard his music hundreds or even thousands of times playing in the game's backdrop, and it never, and I mean NEVER gets tiring or dull, it really is timeless and beautiful. And it makes me smile a bit whenever I hear it playing somewhere else like someone's youtube video or something as it always brings me back to the quiet moments of the game where I'm playing at a cold rainy night, just travelling down on some road in-game at night, and I looked up to see the night skybox and those gorgeous northern lights. That's really the kind of effect the game's soundtracks has on me and I wouldn't have it any other way.

VIRTUAL LARPING, OR UH, VRPING:
As an RPG game, its uhhh, ok-ish? perhaps lackluster? I liked that there's interactive stuff like sitting on chairs and chopping wood on a chopping block, and consume food and drinks, they don't really add to the gameplay but give the players something to act out their roleplay with, which is a net positive in my book, I love opportunities to Larp and play-pretend in a RPG game even if the interactive thing itself doesn't do much in the player's benefit or compromise. After all, RPGs are literally called Role Playing Games. Whenever I build a modlist for this game I tend to value mods that allow me to larp more as I find that to be the most fun way to play. I wish things like alcohol and skooma (in-game universe drug) does more though, they don't really do much but add a temporary and negligible side-effect, getting drunk or high doesn't have any special effects or anything like that sadly.

THE MAIN PREMISE AND ITS EXECUTION:
My biggest personal gripe with the game are the two major events going on in-game, namely the Dragon Crisis and the Civil War. They sound cool as hell on paper and initially hearing about them makes me picture Skyrim going through a major cataclysm, kinsmen against kinsmen divided and fighting amidst the return of the dragons, and with them, the of the end of the world. Sounds apocalyptic and rad right? Welp, these events really lack much presence in the actual game. No real active warzones, and when there are fights there's like six blokes fighting? maybe twelve for the "bigger" fights. Now, seeing as this game is from 2011 I can see why, the hardware they had to work with doesn't allow for such scale quiet yet, but they should at least make the environment reflect that there is war cuz most places looked serene. It's pretty cool to see dragons seemingly flying around freely, I just wish they terrorized populated places more though, to y'know, sell them more as a genuine threat. But then again, your average Skyrim grandma npc is gonna chase after it with nothing but a butter knife so I guess Skyrim's citizens don't really care of the danger of these big lizards. And btw, don't kill the chicken on Riverwood when starting out, I got slimed by the local village's populace over it after testing out a bow I yoinked from a corpse, one of my earliest playthroughs of this game got me killed by someone's grandma.

BACK TO LARPING:
Unfortunately the game's quest design heavily limits roleplay potential, lemme explain. 
A good RPG game should allow the player to resolve their quests and obstacles in a variety of ways in accordance to their build, playstyle, and what sort of character they intend to larp as, those who want to be pacifistic should be able to finish a quest without violence and vice versa. This isn't really possible in Skyrim, you inevitably have to kill someone or something. So to call this game an 'RPG' is kinda ehhhh.... The term 'Action-RPG' is much more accurate as killing is gonna be the main way you'll get around and make progress. Side tangent here but I ironically I do enjoy Skyrim more as a cozy game more than something like Stardew Valley because you can take it at your own pace and not have to worry about anything time-sensitive, that and it's stellar atmosphere.

LEVELING UP:
It's braindead simple, it's just "Do x to improve x skill" and it'll level up, level up your skills enough and your main character level goes up and grants you a perk point to apply on any of the skill perk trees. It looks and sounds cool at first and its presented beautifully, but its depth is about as deep as a puddle. The perks are deadass boring honestly, I'd recommend a perk overhaul mod. A good chunk of vanilla perks are just "Do/Add 20% more something" and the marginally more interesting ones will take a while to reach. 

COMBAT:
It's uh, hardly the best. Which is not good for a game where combat takes up a chunk of what you'll mainly be doing. The melee combat is braindead basic, click for basic slash, long click for a power attack, block to reduce damage, and bash while blocking to stagger your foe. I probably won't mind this as much if the combat feedback looks and feels good but it doesn't. Makes me wish the game's melee combat has a more complex system like Mordhau but I digress. I don't really touch on magic and sneaking as much but as far as I can see they seem as one-dimensional too, which in other words means they're as basic and simple as melee combat. But they can be more fun than taking up the floaty melee fights at least.

One good thing I REALLY like about Skyrim's combat is the ability to equip any pair of spells, weapons, or staffs in either of your hands. You can be a spellsword and have any one-handed spell on one hand while the other wields a one-handed blade, or have two different separate spells in your two hands, or even dual wield bladed weapons.

RANDOM STUFF:
I like horses as animals but horse riding in this game feels pretty ass. Poor Lydia has to chase after me on foot as I ride, why can't she ride with me? Or at least the option to buy her a steed of her own.

The questlines for the game's guilds are uhh, something? Know that you'll turn into a fresh-faced member to its leader surprisingly quick.

Shouts are fun, Fuz ro dah is super fun, but there are shouts that I hardly use at all as they seem pretty impractical or unuseful. One of the coolest shouts in the game forces the weather to change into a deadly thunder storm, but it can zap a friendly npc and suddenly I'm being chased by angry people who were on my side not long ago.
Fun, addictive as hell, and I finally understand the memes now lol

Not sure any more positive reviews are even needed - the game speaks for itself.
One of the best games I have ever purchased, full of action, exploration, and fun. No matter what your playstyle is, you will find a way to love this game. There really is something for everyone, whether you wanna fight, complete quests, or just forage mushrooms and become an alchemist. 
The nostalgia on this one is unparalleled. It will never lose it's spark, and that is special for such an old game.
Great exploration game, the story doesnt beat oblivion but it keeps up I feel like it has the best mods potential and it creates non stop fun the more you explore!

While I've only played this game on steam for 20+ hours. I've also played 1000+ hours on playstation. This game holds a special place in my heart and I would recommend it to others. The best part about this game is that there is so much to do that even with all my playtime, I still haven't seen everything skyrim has to offer.

# The Elder Scrolls V: Skyrim Special Edition Review — Still One of My Favorite Games

Skyrim is one of those games that never really leaves my rotation. I have over 200 hours in the Steam version alone, and that does not even count the time I have put into it on consoles over the years. At this point, it is not just a game I played. It is one of my favorite games.

What makes Skyrim special is the feeling of stepping into a world and being allowed to go almost anywhere. You can follow the main story, ignore it completely, join factions, hunt dragons, explore caves, collect gear, build a character, roleplay, mod the game, or just wander until something interesting happens. That freedom is still the strongest part of the experience.

The world itself is what keeps pulling me back. Skyrim has forests, mountains, snowy ruins, ancient tombs, cities, small towns, hidden caves, strange quests, and random moments that somehow still feel memorable after hundreds of hours. Even when you think you know the map, you can still stumble into something you forgot about or find yourself starting a new character just to experience the world in a different way.

The gameplay is not perfect, and honestly, Skyrim has always had its rough edges. Combat can be clunky, some systems are unbalanced, bugs are part of the experience, and the game definitely shows its age in places. But despite all of that, it still works. The sense of adventure is strong enough that the flaws rarely ruin the experience for me.

The Special Edition helps keep the game feeling playable today, especially with better visuals, stability improvements, and mod support. Mods are a huge part of Skyrim’s lifespan. Whether you want better graphics, new quests, quality-of-life improvements, survival elements, combat changes, or just ridiculous nonsense, the modding scene makes the game feel almost endless.

But even without mods, Skyrim still has something that a lot of games struggle to capture: atmosphere. Walking through the wilderness at night, hearing the music swell, seeing a dragon circle overhead, finding a random dungeon, or coming across a weird little story in the middle of nowhere still hits. It is the kind of game where the journey often matters more than the objective.

For me, Skyrim is comfort gaming. It is the game I can come back to when I want adventure without overthinking it. It is familiar, but still open enough to feel fresh. I can take it seriously, mess around, roleplay, explore, or just get lost for a few hours.

My verdict is an easy recommendation.

Skyrim is not flawless, but it is legendary for a reason. It is one of the best open-world RPGs ever made, and even after all these years, it still has a magic that keeps me coming back.

Over 200 hours on Steam, even more on consoles, and I still know I will end up playing it again.

Final Verdict: One of My Favorites
